video_title: Why Smart Investors Still Make Costly Mistakes
gist: Mental performance coach Evan of M1 Performance Group, a former money manager who left Wall Street at 46 after a panic attack, tells Wealthion that ~90% of investing outcomes are psychological: the edge comes from moving from reactive to responsive decision-making, clearing the nervous system's residue between trades, and reframing regret over missed moves like the recent gold and silver runs. Despite the 'long' metadata tag this is a ~27-minute behavioral-psychology interview with no market forecasts or tickers.

insights.claim: Evan of M1 Performance Group argues no decision is made without emotion, but for most investors emotions are tied to past programming and produce reactive rather than responsive choices; the coaching goal is to create space, in the face of any emotion, to pick conscious behavior.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group, ex-money manager)
insights.so_what: Frames trading errors as reactive impulses; the actionable lever is pausing to choose behavior rather than trying to suppress emotion.
insights.quote: impulsivity is chaos. That's reacting. Intuition is calmness. That's responding.

insights.claim: Evan warns experienced investors who assume age has made them 'intuitive' to first understand their impulsivity before trusting intuition, because what feels like a seasoned gut read is often an unexamined reaction.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: A check against overconfident 'gut' trades from veteran investors: distinguish calm intuition from chaotic impulse before acting.
insights.quote: understand your impulsivities first before you you trust your intuition

insights.claim: Evan prescribes asking yourself 'self-exploratory questions' out loud (e.g. 'What am I trying to accomplish?', 'Why am I reacting and not responding?') because verbalizing them creates mental space that shifts you from reacting to responding.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: A concrete, no-cost pre-trade routine to interrupt impulsive execution.
insights.quote: it has to come out of your mouth. You just can't think this. It creates mental space

insights.claim: Evan contends behavioral patterns are not permanent: repeatedly tying a new conscious behavior to a recurring emotion like fear builds new experiences that rewire neural circuitry (neuroplasticity), so investors are not locked into destructive habits.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Reframes chronic behavioral mistakes as fixable through deliberate repetition rather than innate temperament.
insights.quote: if I consistently take an emotion, fear of whatever, tie a new behavior to it, the experience is going to be different

insights.claim: Evan holds that behavior changes before feelings and that emotions cannot be directly controlled, so the practical starting point for change is altering how you respond, not trying to change how you feel.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Directs effort toward controllable actions (process, sizing, routines) rather than unproductive attempts to feel differently.

insights.claim: Evan describes the brain as a predicting mechanism and the source of anxiety, arguing that feeding new positive experiences into that 'predictive pond' changes the whole equation of how one anticipates and reacts.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Explains anxiety around uncertain outcomes as prediction, and offers deliberate new experience as the counter.
insights.quote: the brain is a predicting mechanism. That's where anxiety comes from.

insights.claim: Evan's core framework is Performance = Potential minus Interferences: interferences never disappear entirely, but reducing them frees energy to build more potential, which is what shifts performance higher.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: A diagnostic lens: work on removing psychological interference rather than only adding skill or data.

insights.claim: Evan argues success itself creates a hidden blind spot: while things go well, small 'kinks in the foundation' develop unnoticed, so staying successful requires periodic self-reflection to 'look under the hood.'
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Warns wealthy, previously successful investors that a good track record can mask deteriorating process.
insights.quote: what got you here is not going to get you where you need to go

insights.claim: Evan stresses that a best decision does not guarantee a best outcome and only time reveals if you were right; the edge comes from stacking best decisions repeatedly so probability, not any single trade, ends up in your favor.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Separates process from outcome, discouraging results-based self-judgment on individual trades.
insights.quote: one best decisions doesn't mean you're going to have a best outcome

insights.claim: Evan argues that two investors with identical data and the same trade idea can have opposite P&L, so the decisive differentiator is the mental edge rather than the information itself.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Reframes 'I did all the research and still lost' as an execution/psychology problem, not an analysis gap.
insights.quote: It all becomes little the mental edge is everything.

insights.claim: Evan disputes the textbook claim that every trade is an independent event: intellectually it should be, but the nervous system carries residue from each prior decision, so investors must deliberately clear to a near 'zero-zero mindset' before the next trade.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Explains tilt and revenge trading; the practical skill is resetting between decisions rather than assuming a clean slate.
insights.quote: our nervous systems carry residue from each decision we make. Just knowing that is powerful.

insights.claim: Evan identifies the ability to reset, down-regulate and create space after a loss as one of the most valuable and underrated professional skills, one that people wrongly assume happens automatically.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Points to recovery/recalibration as a trainable edge, especially through drawdowns and stressful regimes.
insights.quote: how do you recalibrate to come back again? That is one of the most important things to learn right there.

themes.theme: Regret and missed moves (gold and silver)

insights.claim: On gold's roughly 40-50% run and subsequent ~20% pullback that left many Wealthion viewers feeling they missed selling, Evan reframes the choice around pre-structuring the position sizing so you own some and sell some, accepting Buffett's idea that it may be 'your worst sell ever,' rather than ruminating over top-ticking.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: A behavioral playbook for managing regret in extended commodity moves: size to avoid regret in either direction ahead of time.
insights.quote: how will you feel if you don't be a if you're not a part of this?

insights.claim: Evan shares that he had held silver since 2008 and sold most of his position around $92, after which silver rose toward $130, and he treats that as a well-executed decision to accept rather than a source of rumination.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Models detaching self-worth from top-ticking; a sold winner that keeps running is not a failure of process.
insights.quote: I was in silver since '08. And I think I sold most of my position at 92. And then it obviously went much higher.

insights.claim: Evan advises holding a structured way of thinking but staying flexible within that structure rather than rigid, because rigidity behaves like scarcity and shrinks you while flexibility opens up clarity and space to respond to changing markets.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: A guard against clinging to a plan; distinguishes disciplined structure from brittle rigidity.
insights.quote: Think about rigidity as as scarcity, as you get smaller. Flexibility, you get to open up

insights.claim: Evan reframes confidence as knowing you don't know everything and warns that anchoring to a single past reference point shuts investors off from adapting to ever-changing markets.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Ties overconfidence and fixed reference prices to failure to adapt; curiosity is the corrective.
insights.quote: confidence is knowing that you don't know everything

themes.theme: Learning in success and process over win rate

insights.claim: Evan argues investors focus on learning from struggle but neglect learning in success; because success constantly moves you into uncharted territory, the discipline is to 'recommit' to the basics that got you there rather than take them for granted.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Counsels reviewing and reinforcing process precisely when results are good, before decay sets in.
insights.quote: When you're successful, you are constantly putting yourself in uncharted territories.

insights.claim: Evan says his best trading year had only a 20% win rate but a massive slugging ratio, arguing you are 'allowed to be wrong' frequently as long as you size and manage winners well, and that win-loss ratio is a misleading scorecard.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Reinforces asymmetric payoff investing: hit rate matters far less than magnitude of winners versus losers.
insights.quote: my best year my win-loss ratio was 20%. I go, But, my slugging ratio was massive.

insights.claim: Evan asserts that roughly 90% of investing performance is mental rather than about the analysis, comparing surface-level explanations to a 'leaf on a tree' when the real driver is always the 'roots' of one's mental makeup.
insights.speaker: Evan (M1 Performance Group)
insights.so_what: Sets priorities: allocate effort to psychology and process, not just deeper research, to improve results.
insights.quote: for your listeners, to know that 90% of this is mental.
